method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is Japanese for “western island”.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was known as Rosario Island before it was renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The name is sometimes romanized with a hyphen as Nishino-shima.[5]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Islands.[4] It lies on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge, the volcanic front of the Izu–Bonin arc, which trends roughly north–south parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanism in the region is caused by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The island forms the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the main peak exposed above sea level, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeastern flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]

Nishinoshima has historically been described as an andesitic volcanic island, with ejecta from the 1973 activity containing 51.8–60.1 wt% SiO₂.[1] Submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption show a broader compositional range, including basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite.[3] The andesites are similar to material erupted from the island since 1973, and their primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] The 2020 basalts resemble older basalts previously identified in surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma may have been generated throughout Nishinoshima’s history but erupted at the island summit in 2020.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the island’s southern tip, and by September it had formed a new island.[1] The eruption began with submarine phreatomagmatic explosions and migrated semicircularly, with five craters producing volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] Although erosion removed much of the newly formed volcanic edifices, reworked deposits connected the new land to the older island and closed the mouth of the intervening bay by 1982.[1] During the 1973–1974 activity, multiple new islets appeared, coalesced above sea level, and were named Nishinoshima Shintō by the Maritime Safety Agency in December 1973.[1]

The 2013–2020 eruptive period has been divided into four episodes, with durations of about two years for Episode 1, 120 days for Episode 2, one week for Episode 3, and 270 days for Episode 4.[3] Average lava effusion rates were estimated at 2.0 × 10⁵ m³/day for the 2013–2015 eruption and 1.5 × 10⁵ m³/day for the 2017 eruption.[3] Monitoring of these eruptions relied on satellites, aircraft, and research vessels, including optical imagery and synthetic-aperture radar data capable of observing the island through clouds and eruption plumes.[4] Episode 4 began with Strombolian activity and lava effusion, shifted to lava-fountain eruptions, and ended with ash emission probably generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[4] This transition to abundant fine ash production had not been observed in previous Nishinoshima eruptions and has been compared with the late explosive phase of the 2000 Miyakejima eruption.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is an important natural laboratory for observing primary succession, species colonization and ecosystem development on newly created oceanic land.[3][4] Its terrestrial ecology is strongly shaped by recurrent volcanic disturbance, with eruptions capable of removing most vegetation and repeatedly resetting early successional communities.[2][5] Early botanical work documented plant invasion on new volcanic ground at Nishinoshima, making the island useful for studying how plants establish on isolated, recently formed substrates.[4]

The island is part of the Ogasawara archipelago, whose isolated oceanic ecosystems are noted for high levels of endemism, adaptive radiation and ongoing speciation.[5] Compared with the older and more biologically developed Ogasawara islands, Nishinoshima’s biota is especially dynamic because available habitat is frequently altered by volcanic activity and coastal erosion.[5] Research on Nishinoshima includes monitoring rates of species colonization and the development of surrounding marine communities, including coral reef formation.[3]

One documented example of ecological loss on the island is the disappearance of the Nishinoshima population of common purslane, *Portulaca oleracea*, after volcanic activity in 2019.[2] Before that eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected living purslane plants from the island for laboratory cultivation, preserving material from a population later reported to have become locally extinct.[2] Although *P. oleracea* occurs widely in tropical regions, its loss from Nishinoshima illustrates how small, isolated island populations can be eliminated by episodic volcanic disturbance.[2]
